MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Jun-29-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: DEFENDANT WEATHERSTORM CAPITAL, LLC MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

Appearances:

Anthony Schoenberg Esq., and Christopher Wheeler Esq., in person, Farella, Braun & Martel, for Plaintiffs.

J. Scott Humphrey, Esq., HUMPHREY + LAW, (310) 890-5326, for Defendant SHIRISH MALEKAR.

Not contested/argued. The court adopts the following tentative ruling:

Defendant Weatherstorm Capital, LLC's (Weatherstorm) motion for summary adjudication is denied. A motion for summary adjudication shall be granted if all the papers submitted show that there is no triable issue as to any material fact and that the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. (CCP sec. 437c(c).) The moving party has the initial burden of production to show the nonexistence of any triable issue of material fact; if he carries his burden of production, he causes a shift, and the opposing party is then subjected to a burden of production of his own to show the existence of a triable issue of material fact. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 850.) "On application by or for a member or manager the Court of Chancery may decree dissolution of a limited liability company whenever it is not reasonably practicable to carry on the business in conformity with a limited liability company agreement.? (6 Del. C. sec. 18-802.) Delaware case law provides that it is not reasonably practicable when: (1) the members' vote is deadlocked at the Board level; (2) the operating agreement gives no means of navigating around the deadlock; and (3) due to the financial condition of the company, there is effectively no business to operate. (Fisk Ventures, LLC v. Segal (2009) 2009 WL 73957 at *4.) A triable issue of material fact exists as to Weatherstorm's solvency. Shirish Malekar testifies that Weatherstorm has $1,065,706.25 in cash. (Shirish Decl. para 14.) But this, along with other material facts regarding Weatherstorm's finances, are disputed. (See UMF # 18-20.) The Doctors Company provides multiple profit and loss statements by Weatherstorm showing negative net income. (See Additional Material Facts A1-A6.) Weatherstorm's financial condition is relevant to the consideration of whether it is "reasonably practicable" for the business to carry on. (See 6 Del. C. sec. 18-802.) Because there is a triable issue of material fact, summary adjudication to the third cause of action for judicial dissolution is denied. (Edmon & Karnow, CALIFORNIA PRACTICE GUIDE: CIVIL PROCEDURE BEFORE TRIAL para 10:270 (Rutter 2022).)

WAUKEEN Q. McCOY, ESQ., McCOY LAW FIRM, P.C, (415) 675-7705, for Defendant, SHIRISH MALEKAR
Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com Court reporter

Hearing is held. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

DEFENDANT WEATHERSTORM CAPITAL, LLC DEMURRER to COMPLAINT

Defendant Weatherstorm Capital, LLC's ("Weatherstorm's") demurrer is overruled as to Plaintiff The Doctors Company's ("TDC's") third cause of action for dissolution.

Section 7.1 of the parties' LLC Agreement provides, "[t]he Company shall be dissolved and its affairs shall be wound up upon (c) Entry of a decree of judicial dissolution pursuant to the [Delaware Limited Liability Company] Act." (Def.'s RJN, Ex. 1 to Ex A.) The Delaware Limited Liability Company Act provides, "the Court of Chancery may decree dissolution of a limited liability company whenever it is not reasonably practicable to carry on the business in conformity with a limited liability company agreement." (6 Del. Code sec. 18-802 (emphasis added).)

Based on the foregoing, Weatherstorm argues that section 7.1(c) grants exclusive jurisdiction to Delaware courts to enter decrees of judicial dissolution. This argument is without merit.

First, California Superior Courts are courts of general jurisdiction. They lack jurisdiction only in cases within the exclusive jurisdiction of federal courts and in controversies that must be heard by other tribunals. (Weil & Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Guide), Ch. 3-A, 4.) Neither limitation is present here: decrees of judicial dissolution are not within the exclusive jurisdiction of federal courts, and the Delaware Limited Liability Company Act merely permits Delaware courts to enter decrees of judicial dissolution.

In any case, section 7.1(c) is a choice of law provision, not a jurisdictional one. While section 9.5 is a general choice of law provision requiring that Delaware law be applied to substantive claims arising from the LLC Agreement, section 7.1(c) is a more specific choice of law provision that indicates exactly which Delaware law should govern dissolution claims.

Furthermore, the LLC Agreement contains clear jurisdictional consent: "The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally agree that all claims with respect to any such claim may be heard and determined in [the courts of the State of California and the Federal courts of the United States sitting in San Francisco, California.]" (Def.'s RJN, Ex. 1 to Ex A, 9.7(a).) The parties also expressly waived any objection to jurisdiction in San Francisco courts. (Id. at 9.7(b).)

The Court construes the LLC Agreement as a whole. (See City of Atascadero v. Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith (1998) 68 Cal.App.4th 445, 473 ["Any contract must be construed as a whole, with the various individual provisions interpreted together so as to give effect to all, if reasonably possible or practicable."].) Therefore, the Court finds that it has subject matter jurisdiction over TDC's dissolution claim and must apply substantive Delaware law (specifically, the Delaware Limited Liability Company Act) to resolve this claim.

Order is signed.

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/SRB)
